topics.answer: In the cited laboratory assay, female Drosophila suzukii preferred the harder oviposition texture. Drugs targeting TRP and DEG/ENaC channel families abolished that preference. This separates the observed behavior from the candidate mechanism: mechanosensitive channels are implicated, but the experiment did not identify one uniquely causal channel. It also does not show that fruit hardness alone determines field infestation or that one channel is a proven commercial target. If a candidate and its vehicle were applied to substrates with different firmness, the treatment contrast cannot isolate odor. Match firmness across treatments or cross candidate versus vehicle with at least two pre-specified firmness levels in a factorial design, measure firmness rather than assigning it by label, and analyze treatment, firmness, and their interaction. This is an R&D control derived from the texture confound, not a protocol tested by the cited paper.

In the cited artificial-substrate assay, Drosophila suzukii laid fewer eggs on soft 1% agar with visible commensal microbial growth than on the matched uninoculated substrate. By contrast, Drosophila melanogaster and Drosophila biarmipes preferred the microbe-rich soft substrate and laid more eggs there. On hard 3% agar, D. suzukii showed no significant preference or aversion to microbial growth. The hardness-dependent change implicates an interaction between mechanosensory and chemosensory information; it does not identify the responsible microbial molecule or show that every fruit microbe deters SWD in crops. To isolate the interaction, cross inoculated versus uninoculated substrate with matched soft versus hard levels, randomize sides, and analyze the interaction. Measure both egg allocation in choice assays and total egg output in matched no-choice assays so a site preference is not confused with a change in overall oviposition. These are R&D controls, not additional results from the paper.

The paper reported CFFA as an oviposition deterrent. Advance the caprylic-capric blend only as a bounded formulation hypothesis, not as a finished minimal formulation. The later no-choice comparison counted eggs directly: 20 females oviposited for 24 hours on surface-treated raspberries. At the tested 20 mg CFFA-equivalent composition, each raspberry received 1.38 mg caprylic acid plus 1.46 mg capric acid. Full CFFA and the two-component blend were not significantly different in that assay. CFFA outperformed either acid alone; the blend outperformed capric acid but not caprylic acid. That supports the blend at this composition, dose, substrate, and endpoint, but it does not establish equivalence across doses, release profiles, choice contexts, fruit types, or field conditions. The reported 99% laboratory and 64% field reductions were separate full-CFFA assays based on recovered larvae and pupae, not from direct egg counts and not field tests of the two-component blend. The component choice assays likewise counted recovered larvae and pupae. Recovered offspring combine egg laying with hatch, development, survival, and recovery, so they cannot by themselves isolate oviposition. Because fruit surfaces were treated, none of these assays isolates airborne spatial avoidance from surface contact or fruit chemistry. A defensible down-selection needs component and blend dose-response tests with direct egg counts and total egg output, development and survival follow-up, measured formulation and release chemistry, separate no-contact and surface-contact arms, and replicated fruit and field validation.

The cited comparison separates larval nutrition from adult oviposition. Drosophila suzukii larvae performed better at lower protein concentrations and consumed less protein-rich diets than Drosophila biarmipes. Adult egg laying did respond to nutritional composition: both species preferentially laid eggs on the 1:8 protein-to-carbohydrate diet and laid fewer eggs on the 1:1 diet. The species did not differ significantly in their nutritional preference, so the study did not find a species-specific adult preference that mirrored their different larval nutritional performance. Their hardness choices did differ: D. biarmipes preferred the softest tested substrate, while D. suzukii showed no significant hardness preference. In product assays, manipulate texture and nutrition independently and keep larval performance, larval intake, adult egg allocation, and total fecundity as separate endpoints.

Both adult density and host quality changed Drosophila suzukii oviposition in the cited laboratory experiments, but substrate quality affected oviposition more than adult density. Whole raspberries supported substantially more oviposition than lower-quality raspberry agar. On whole raspberries, eggs per female declined as female density increased; on raspberry agar, intermediate densities produced the highest per-female oviposition. Male density did not significantly change oviposition on whole raspberries, whereas the response to male density on agar was nonlinear. In a separate five-fruit comparison, solitary females laid eggs in about 15% of available fruit while groups of five used about 72%. Because the study did not track individual flies in the grouped treatment, it could not show whether each grouped female spread her eggs more widely. The authors interpreted the results as evidence that social interactions can encourage oviposition, especially on unfamiliar or unnatural substrates. Total eggs, eggs per female, and fruit coverage are different outcomes and should not be collapsed. If treatment groups contain different numbers of flies, match female and male density, host quality, and host distribution before attributing an egg difference to the candidate. A lower eggs-per-female value after increasing fly number does not establish oviposition deterrence. These laboratory density and substrate effects do not directly predict field infestation.

The study tested 2-phenylethanol at 0.01-0.1%, ethanol at 1-10%, and acetic acid at 0.1-1%. In Y-tube choices, Drosophila melanogaster favored the higher tested concentrations more strongly, whereas Drosophila suzukii tended toward lower concentrations. Electroantennogram responses measured antennal detection separately from choice. Mated females favored higher concentrations more than virgins only in the D. melanogaster comparison, so that mating-state result cannot be assigned to SWD. Keep chemical identity, concentration, Y-tube behavior, and electroantennogram response separate; the study does not show that all fermentation volatiles repel SWD.

Female summer- and winter-morph Drosophila suzukii differed in both antennal and behavioral responses. Isoamyl acetate, acetic acid, and geosmin produced significantly larger electroantennogram responses in summer morphs, while winter-morph responses were reduced overall. Geosmin and bornyl acetate also produced morph-dependent behavior in no-choice assays; in the geosmin T-maze, summer morphs showed significant aversion while winter morphs showed no significant aversion. A weaker bulk antennal response is not proof that winter morphs cannot detect an odor, and summer-morph aversion cannot be assigned to winter morphs. Screen both morphs at matched delivered doses under matched temperature, light, and assay geometry, and keep antennal response, behavioral valence, locomotion, survival, recovery, oviposition, and field efficacy separate.

No. Scoring treatment and control at different times confounds a chemical effect with Drosophila suzukii's daily egg-laying rhythm. Hourly oviposition was 2.4-fold higher in light than dark at constant 23 degrees C, 15.4-fold higher under a 17:7 light-dark cycle with 17/11 degrees C, and 9.1-fold and 25-fold higher in the August and October field assays. Field peaks were not fixed to one sunrise or sunset interval, and oviposition dipped when August field temperatures exceeded 30 degrees C. Repeat treatment and control concurrently when possible, or randomize both within replicated time blocks while matching fruit, fly state, light, temperature, humidity, and exposure duration. Keep egg-laying rhythm, locomotor activity, treatment effect, and egg-to-adult survival as separate endpoints; do not transfer one month's or one laboratory regime's peak to another.

Methyl jasmonate was not uniformly repellent. In the cited planar olfactometer, 309.0 micrograms per filter paper was repellent, while 3.86 to 15.45 micrograms per filter paper was attractive. In a separate two-choice cage, doses above 1287.5 micrograms per filter paper reduced oviposition by more than 90 percent on artificial fruits. Run separate carrier-controlled, log-spaced series for the two endpoints: bracket the orientation reversal from below 3.86 through 309.0 micrograms per filter paper, and bracket the oviposition transition around 1287.5 micrograms per filter paper in the cage assay. Measure release rate and air concentration over time before translating either result beyond its apparatus. Applied mass per filter paper is not measured air concentration or a crop application rate, and neither endpoint by itself proves field crop protection.

topics.answer: Fewer eggs on a treated choice cannot by itself establish sensory repellency. Interpret egg allocation alongside total eggs or a no-choice fecundity measure, survival, locomotion or dwell behavior, and standardized mating and physiological state. The cited pre-exposure study separately measured trap capture, female survival, and oviposition frequency, illustrating why one egg endpoint is insufficient. Choice, activity, toxicity, mating, and fecundity must remain separate explanations.

Measure orientation and egg laying as separate endpoints on separate timelines. In a non-contact airflow arena, prevent surface contact and record approach, heading, entry, zone occupancy, exit, dwell time, speed, distance, and immobility at fixed intervals. In parallel contact-permitted choice and no-choice oviposition assays, count both egg allocation and total eggs over time, then move flies to clean untreated substrate and measure post-exposure catch-up. An immediate directional shift away from the odor with normal locomotion before egg laying supports spatial avoidance. Normal approach and occupancy followed by later egg deposition, reduced total egg output, or catch-up after odor removal supports delayed or suppressed oviposition instead. Use carrier and airflow shams, reverse treatment side, randomize order, and standardize age, mating, feeding, prior egg laying, substrate, and circadian time. Track knockdown, survival, and recovery separately so motor impairment is not mislabeled as repellency. This measurement chain distinguishes the endpoints; it does not by itself prove field crop protection.

Use both a matched untreated control and a carrier-only or solvent-only control. Match airflow direction and speed, ventilation, source position, and release rate between sides; run a clean-air sham, randomize or reverse treatment side and order, and use independent biological replicates. To distinguish directed odor avoidance from impaired movement, separately measure heading or zone choice alongside flight or walking speed, total distance, immobility, takeoff, knockdown, survival, and recovery in matched clean air. Preserve total eggs or a matched no-choice fecundity endpoint so reallocation between sides is not mistaken for reduced oviposition. Standardize age, sex, mating, feeding, prior egg laying, substrate condition, and circadian time. Selective orientation away from the odor with normal clean-air locomotion supports repellency; slowing or immobility in both settings supports impairment instead. A two-choice laboratory result still does not prove field crop protection.

A Y-tube or planar olfactometer measures orientation or choice under that apparatus. It does not by itself measure egg laying, larval survival, fruit damage, or marketable yield. In the methyl jasmonate study, oviposition deterrence was measured separately in a two-choice cage with artificial fruits. Name the actual endpoint before making a product claim and require direct crop and damage outcomes before concluding crop protection.

Match fruit species, cultivar, ripeness, firmness, injury, and surface moisture between treatment and control, or vary them deliberately in a factorial design. Record those variables and randomize or block them across positions. The cited blueberry experiment showed that fruit injury directly changed oviposition and interacted hierarchically with other cues. It did not measure a specific moisture effect, so moisture control is an experimental-design recommendation rather than that paper's result.

Follow eggs with larval or pupal emergence, fruit infestation or damage, and marketable yield or loss in replicated field conditions. The cited push-pull study found fewer pupae emerging from treated raspberries, which is stronger than an egg-only endpoint. It still does not provide the complete package of crop damage, marketable yield, persistence, and operational fit needed for a commercial crop-protection claim.

source_provenance.locator: Abstract and field raspberry methods/results; laboratory choice and no-choice assays; Figure 5 pupae emerging from ripening and sentinel raspberries under control, pull, push, and push-pull treatments

topics.answer: Untreated Aedes aegypti host seeking combines carbon dioxide, skin odor, visual contrast, thermal cues, and other close-range information rather than one signal. In the op1/op2 double mutants, carbon-dioxide-stimulated visual target attraction was nearly eliminated at 1000 and 100 lux and abolished at 25 and 5 lux, while human-odor tracking remained normal. This light-dependent result shows separable visual and odor pathways; it is not an unqualified loss of visual attraction under every light level. The arm-in-cage study also shows that contact and bite endpoints can diverge. None of this proves which channels remain intact after a particular repellent: compound-specific exposure must be tested while keeping activation, orientation, landing, contact, probing, and biting distinct.

Two 1979 primary studies support distinct post-blood-meal processes. The abdominal-distention study found that a replete blood meal and saline enemas immediately inhibited host seeking. The oocyte-maturation study separated egg development from feeding by using blood enemas; females that developed eggs were inhibited during oocyte maturation, and surgical manipulation plus haemolymph transfer implicated a circulating inhibitory factor. A 2013 genetic study describes roughly three days of suppression, treats Head Peptide-I as a candidate signal, and shows that NPYLR1 is not required. Standardizing feeding status, time since feeding, abdominal distention, egg-development state, and mating or insemination state is an R&D recommendation, not a universal protocol established by these studies. The evidence does not establish one receptor, the anatomical location of a distention sensor, or a universal 24-hour phase across the gonotrophic cycle.

No. NPYLR1 responded to Head Peptide-I, but NPYLR1 null mutants still showed normal post-blood-meal host-seeking suppression. In that study NPYLR1 was therefore not required in vivo. The result does not distinguish redundant signaling through other receptors from signaling through a different unknown receptor. Ligand or receptor activation is not the same as genetic necessity, and this result should not be confused with later work on NPYLR7 agonists.

source_provenance.locator: Results: NPYLR1 response to Head Peptide-I and sNPFs; NPYLR1 null-mutant host-seeking suppression experiment and conclusion that NPYLR1 is not required in vivo

topics.answer: Measure detection and avoidance independently at the same delivered dose. First test peripheral or central sensory responses with a solvent blank, a known-odor positive control, and a candidate dose-response; an electroantennogram is a broad antennal measure, so single-sensillum or calcium recordings may be needed to localize a response. Separately, use a non-contact choice, flight, or landing assay and record approach, occupancy, exit, and landing while also measuring locomotion, knockdown, survival, and recovery. An intact neural response without avoidance shows detection without demonstrated aversive behavior; it can also reflect a response below the behavioral threshold or the wrong context. No measured neural response plus no avoidance is only consistent with failure of the measured pathway if the positive controls and movement remain intact; it is not proof that every sensory route failed. For causality, disrupt the candidate receptor or pathway in a matched genetic background and rescue it, then repeat both neural and behavioral tests. The cited Aedes aegypti study illustrates the logic: a high-purity transfluthrin preparation reduced landings in a non-contact hand-in-cage assay at a 1 ppm treatment-solution concentration without a detectable whole-antenna electroantennogram signal, and repellency persisted in orco mutants but fell in sodium-channel kdr mutants. DEET showed a different Orco-dependent pattern. Thus a behavioral effect, antennal signal, receptor response, or mutation alone cannot establish the whole detection-to-avoidance mechanism.

The whole-antenna electroantennogram detected no response to the tested high-purity Sigma-Aldrich transfluthrin preparation, reported as 99.2-99.9% purity, even when it was delivered undiluted. That null bulk recording does not exclude a response in individual antennal neurons, another sensory organ, or downstream neural circuitry. DEET, 1-octen-3-ol, and lactic acid produced the expected electroantennogram positive control signals. In a separate non-contact hand-in-cage assay, an untreated upper net prevented mosquitoes from touching the treated lower net or the hand. A 1 ppm treatment solution, reported by the authors as 20 ng/cm2 applied to the lower net, reduced Aedes aegypti landings; the study did not measure airborne concentration at the mosquito position. No locomotor abnormality was reported at 1 ppm or below during the assay, and early monitoring found no abnormal behavior over one hour or mortality over 24 hours after those exposures. Uncoordinated movement appeared at 100 ppm and some knockdown at 1000 ppm. Transfluthrin repellency persisted in orco-null mosquitoes while DEET repellency was reduced. Transfluthrin repellency was lower in KDR:ROCK mosquitoes carrying S989P and V1016G sodium-channel mutations, while DEET repellency was not lower; the authors noted that unknown background differences between KDR:ROCK and Rockefeller could still exist. Converging evidence came from the channel and stereoisomer tests: the mutant mosquito sodium channel was less sensitive to transfluthrin, and the inactive 1S-cis isomer produced neither sodium-channel tail current in Xenopus oocytes nor repellency. Together, the results support a sodium-channel-dependent principal mechanism for this tested high-purity preparation without a detectable whole-antenna electroantennogram response and without requiring intact Orco-mediated odorant-receptor signaling. They do not establish the complete detection-to-avoidance pathway. The measured endpoint was laboratory landing, not biting, infection, disease prevention, or open-field protection. Three tested commercial transfluthrin products behaved differently and included an Orco-dependent component, so their mechanism cannot be inferred from the high-purity preparation alone. That commercial-product result does not show that transfluthrin itself has two receptor mechanisms. The products contained unidentified additional compounds, so the Orco-dependent component cannot be assigned to transfluthrin, a specific additive, or an interaction among formulation components from this study.

A 150-person cohort showed differential Aedes aegypti attraction and preliminary models in which measured blood metabolites contributed to explained variation. Histidine-pathway metabolites were negatively correlated with attraction, while long-chain fatty acids and monoacylglycerols were positively correlated. These are associations, not proof that changing a metabolite changes attraction. For repellent assays, treat donor identity and sampling day as design variables: repeat collections, balance donor order across treatments, include multiple donors, and use donor as a blocking or repeated-measures factor. Keep blood metabolites separate from skin emissions, receptor responses, odor-sample choice, landing, and biting. A separate human-odor study found stable attraction differences across repeated samples from the same people over months to years; that supports repeated donor sampling but still does not make the metabolite correlations causal.

If mosquitoes briefly touch treated skin before bites decline, the result does not prove repellency at a distance because physical contact already occurred. In the cited tracking study, topical repellents reduced bites even though mosquitoes made multiple brief skin contacts; that separates contact from biting but does not identify whether the post-contact effect was irritancy, taste-mediated deterrence, or another mechanism. Operationally, spatial repellency is movement away or reduced entry without physical contact, while contact irritancy is locomotor excitation or escape after touching treatment. A paired excito-repellency design separates non-contact and contact exposure and should also record knockdown and mortality. To establish a distance effect, prevent contact with a barrier and measure entry, orientation, landing, or escape before any touch, alongside a matched contact arm. Escape from a contact chamber does not prove spatial repellency, and reduced bites or landings alone do not establish an olfactory mechanism.

Use paired non-contact treatment and carrier-only control chambers with a mesh barrier that prevents surface contact. Record escape at 1-minute intervals for 30 minutes and compare escape-time curves; that is the direct behavioral measure of spatial avoidance in the cited assay. Run a matched contact format to separate contact excitation from the non-contact response. At 30 minutes, record knockdown separately among escaped and remaining mosquitoes, then follow recovery and 24-hour mortality. In this assay, greater non-contact escape indicates stronger spatial avoidance only when locomotor capacity is preserved. Reduced escape can indicate knockdown or impaired movement and must not be labeled repellency; in a differently designed assay, reduced entry or occupancy can indicate avoidance only when the endpoint direction and motor controls are explicit. Knockdown can suppress escape and mimic a weak behavioral response. Keep spatial avoidance, contact excitation, reversible knockdown, and death as separate endpoints.

The cited transfluthrin study directly supports matched treated and solvent-and-carrier vehicle control chambers, separating contact from non-contact exposure, and keeping escape, knockdown, recovery, and mortality distinct. For environmental reproducibility, it recorded ambient air temperature and relative humidity at the beginning of each 30-minute trial, ran trials during 09:00-16:30 in the same laboratory space under identical conditions, and replicated each trial series four times. It does not publish the numeric assay-room temperature or humidity values. The reported colony conditions were 25 +/- 2 C, 80 +/- 10% relative humidity, and a 12:12 light:dark cycle; those are rearing conditions, not measured assay-room set points. The exposure source was 2.8 mL of transfluthrin solution on 14.7 x 17.5 cm filter paper. Acetone was the solvent and silicone oil the carrier at a reported 1.91:0.95 ratio; control papers used both without transfluthrin. Papers were air-dried at room temperature on aluminium foil for 24 hours, used once, and discarded. The paper reports initial loading but says active ingredient remaining on those papers at test time was not chemically determined. Separately, it does not report chamber-air concentration, plume structure, airflow direction and speed, a measured source release rate, or delivery-hardware characterization beyond the treated paper; that is an airborne-exposure measurement gap, not the paper's named treated-paper residue limitation. This paper therefore cannot by itself define a complete product-specific exposure package. For a new assay, record numeric chamber temperature and humidity over the full trial, define and monitor airflow direction and speed, sample plume or air concentration where possible, randomize or reverse treatment side, and report vehicle, applied amount, source geometry, drying, release rate, reuse, and delivery hardware. Those are R&D design recommendations, not universal requirements established by this one paper. Keep still-air conclusions separate from directional-flow conclusions and do not treat applied loading alone as airborne dose.

No. Chamber occupancy or escape is an upstream movement endpoint and does not directly measure landing, probing, blood feeding, or biting. In arm-in-cage tracking, topical repellents reduced bites even though mosquitoes made multiple brief skin contacts, showing that encounter, contact, and bite can diverge. A human-protection claim therefore needs a direct human-contact endpoint rather than occupancy alone.

Record immediate knockdown at the end of exposure, repeated recovery or normal-movement status after removal, and delayed mortality such as a 24-hour endpoint. Follow the same exposed cohort and report dead, knocked down, recovered, and behaviorally normal categories against a matched control. A motionless mosquito at exposure end is not necessarily dead, and recovery is not the same endpoint as survival or voluntary avoidance during exposure.

Table 8 labels N=6 and reports a mean Aedes aegypti protection time of 4.0 +/- 0.0 hours for F3, a 10 percent total citronella formulation containing a 1:1 mixture of free oil and citronella-loaded nanostructured lipid carrier. F1, 10 percent free citronella oil in the same oil-in-water emulsion base, averaged 0.3 +/- 0.5 hours. However, Methods says that four formulations were evaluated on three participants, so the human sample size is unresolved and the table label must not be silently converted into six distinct volunteers. The study measured skin permeation and thermal properties, but it did not directly measure volatile release rate. The authors inferred prolonged release from the formulation, thermal behavior, permeation and mass-balance evidence, and efficacy. It does not show that every controlled-release citronella formulation protects for four hours, nor does it establish durability through sweat, washing, abrasion, varied climates, natural use, or prevention of mosquito-borne disease.

The evidence is mixed and endpoint-specific. The 2016 receptor-family paper annotated and compared SWD odorant and gustatory receptors, which identifies candidates but does not establish that one receptor causes avoidance. The 2015 avoidance study measured SWD behavior to several odorants, but it did not perturb a specific SWD receptor to prove necessity. The 2026 PNAS study did perform receptor perturbations and found two receptors required for attraction to ripe fruit, while those receptors were dispensable for egg-laying preference; that is causal attraction evidence, not causal repellent avoidance. A specific receptor can be said to drive SWD avoidance only when a selective perturbation changes avoidance to the same compound in the same behavioral assay, with locomotion and toxicity controlled. None of these sources establishes that full chain for a crop-repellent candidate.

Repeat the comparison: visually match treatment and control, or hide the source hardware while randomizing positions. Drosophila suzukii showed color preferences, and the preferred visual cue changed with the accompanying odor in the cited study. The source measured trap-oriented responses, not oviposition, so it does not prove an egg-laying effect. Vision and olfaction should be treated as interacting modalities rather than independent additive cues.

Compare naive and pre-exposed flies at defined exposure and washout times, then repeat the same avoidance, activity, survival, and oviposition endpoints after removing the volatile. The cited study found no loss of deterrence to octenol or 2-pentylfuran after 24, 48, or 72 hours of adult pre-exposure or after larval exposure, but it did not find a geosmin repellent effect in its assay. That compound-specific result does not prove long-term field persistence after removal or weathering.

source_provenance.locator: Abstract: adult female and larval pre-exposure; 24, 48, or 72 hour adult exposures; two-choice gated trap capture, survival, and oviposition endpoints; octenol, geosmin, and 2-pentylfuran results

topics.answer: Do not compare those two assay batches as a treatment-effect contrast because feeding, mating, and gravidity changed together. The cited SWD study used 5- to 10-day-old adults and compared fed flies with flies deprived of food for 15 hours; starvation-induced locomotion changed in ways that depended on sex and microbiome status. In its reproductive-state comparison, conventional virgin females showed lower food seeking than conventional mated gravid females at 7 hours; that difference was not significant at 12 or 24 hours, and axenic virgin and gravid females did not differ significantly at the measured times. The paper also linked lower foraging in axenic females with lower egg production. These direct results show that hunger, bundled reproductive state, microbiome, and assay time can change the behavioral baseline against which a repellent is judged. Age could change reproductive maturity, and prior egg laying could change remaining egg load and oviposition motivation, but this paper did not experimentally measure either effect. Standardize, block, or deliberately vary age, mating, gravidity, feeding, microbiome, time of measurement, and prior egg-laying history, and do not infer unmeasured age or prior-oviposition effect sizes from the reproductive-state result.

source_provenance.title: Sex-Dependent Effects of the Microbiome on Foraging and Locomotion in Drosophila suzukii
source_provenance.locator: Methods: 5- to 10-day-old adults, including axenic and conventional females and males, 15-hour food deprivation, and foraging scored at 7, 12, and 24 hours; Results, Figures 1-3 and reproductive-state comparison: sex- and microbiome-dependent foraging and locomotion, the conventional-female 7-hour virgin-versus-gravid difference and its later time points, axenic comparisons, and oviposition

topics.answer: A crop canopy adds airflow, source spacing and placement, foliage, temperature, timing, and changing release-rate effects that a still-air chamber does not estimate. In 2016 raspberry plots, automated 1-octen-3-ol puffers reduced SWD oviposition by 20% versus controls. In 2017, puffers were 42-55% more effective than passive vials, and dawn and dusk release performed similarly to continuous daytime release. No placement difference was detected, but low fly abundance limited that comparison, so larger-scale perimeter and placement trials remain necessary. The comparison shows that the tested delivery methods differed in outcome under those field conditions. It does not isolate total emitted dose, release rate, airborne concentration at the flies, or another delivery feature as the causal driver. These results are specific to the tested crop, compound, years, pressure, and delivery methods. The next field trial should measure emitted mass and release rate over time; time-resolved canopy concentration at several distances and heights; weather and airflow; source placement and spacing; fruit infestation and damage; and marketable yield under randomized, replicated plots.

Distinguish directed orientation from general motor suppression with separate behavioral and locomotor controls. In the odor plume, measure heading and upwind or downwind movement, turn and exit angle, time inside the plume or treated zone, crosswind displacement, re-entry, and source approach or contact. In matched clean air without the odor plume, measure flight speed, total distance, immobility, takeoff or flight propensity, knockdown, survival, and recovery after exposure. A selective change in trajectory or plume occupancy with preserved locomotion supports an orientation effect; slowing in both plume and clean-air settings supports impairment instead. Randomize side and order, blind tracking, match airflow, solvent, and release rate, and standardize age, sex, mating, feeding, and circadian state. The cited wind-tunnel study scored a combined takeoff-and-upwind-flight response plus source contact in virgin, starved female SWD; it did not report takeoff and upwind flight as separate outcomes. The locomotor study showed that activity varies strongly with mating state and daylight. Together they support the endpoint design, but they do not establish that any candidate volatile is a repellent.

No. The 283.03 m result is the maximum distance recorded from one Drosophila suzukii that flew on a tethered flight mill during an approximately 24-hour laboratory assay; it is not a measured field dispersal distance, plume reach, or crop-protection threshold. Across both apparatuses, the study tested 401 adults and only 15.7% initiated flight: 18.0% in the free-flight chamber and 13.4% on the tethered mill. Apparatus changed behavior. Summer morphs were reported as 14.0% more likely to initiate flight in the chamber than on the mill, and mean first-flight duration was 36.7 seconds in free flight versus 11.7 seconds on the mill. The defensible inference is that tethering and assay geometry materially affect estimated flight propensity and duration, so a single mill maximum cannot define even a minimum field buffer. Set buffer width from direct field evidence that measures movement or mark-release-recapture, plume concentration and persistence across the crop edge, immigration, and fruit infestation or damage at candidate distances. Until those measurements exist, 283.03 m is a laboratory capacity outlier, not a design distance.

No. The cited study compared one Pectis brevipedunculata essential oil in Drosophila suzukii and two pollinator bees, Apis mellifera and Partamona helleri. It used feeding toxicity and diet-consumption endpoints, not a berry-field volatile-repellent exposure. Both bee species were more tolerant than SWD, but exposure at the tested SWD LC50 still killed more bees than the control. Relative tolerance in two species does not establish pollinator safety for a different formulation, dose, route, crop, or exposure pattern. Keep laboratory hazard, expected field exposure, and field risk separate. Before advancing the product as pollinator-safe, test the intended formulation and application rate through relevant airborne, surface-residue, and oral routes in the focal crop and relevant pollinators. Also measure crop injury and quality, residues and worker exposure, field pollinator survival and behavior, predators, parasitoids, soil organisms, and relevant aquatic exposure. The feeding study did not establish any of those wider endpoints, so they remain evidence needs rather than favorable safety results.

Habituation is supported by a within-individual or within-generation decline after nonreinforced exposure that recovers after sufficient washout. Inherited resistance requires a persistent phenotype in offspring raised without exposure and ideally a selection response or genetic association. The cited SWD study found no habituation to octenol or 2-pentylfuran under its adult and larval pre-exposure conditions, but it did not test genetic association, rear unexposed offspring, select lines across generations, measure cross-resistance, or estimate field phenotype frequencies. Use common-garden offspring and unexposed-generation designs to separate learning, parental effects, and inheritance.

Measure eggs per fruit, hatch or larval establishment, pupation or adult emergence, fruit damage and decay, rejected fruit, marketable yield, and economic loss using consistent fruit denominators. Record cultivar, fruit condition, and ripeness as separate factors. The push-pull field work measured fewer pupae emerging from treated raspberries. In the cherry study, the developmental experiment used overripe fruit and found cultivar differences in pupation and survival. Table 1 shows pupation rates of 0.80-0.87 and survival rates of 0.51-0.62. The abstract instead reports pupation up to 0.89; that appears inconsistent with Table 1, where 0.89 is a hatching rate, so the table values are the safer endpoint-specific figures. The study did not compare developmental outcomes across ripeness states. Its ripeness results concern fruit traits and oviposition, so they do not establish a ripeness effect on later development or survival. Every prevented egg would not necessarily have become a damaging larva. Neither cited study measured the complete chain through marketable yield, economic loss, or operational fit, so those outcomes must be measured directly rather than inferred from pupal emergence.

The single and double knockouts separate redundancy from general blindness. Knocking out op1 alone did not impair carbon-dioxide-stimulated visual target selection, and op2 single mutants also behaved like controls. By contrast, op1/op2 double-mutant attraction to the dark target was nearly eliminated at 1000 and 100 lux and abolished at 25 and 5 lux in the cage assay, with severe impairment also seen in the wind tunnel. The double mutants retained odor tracking and normal phototaxis, still showed an optomotor response to optic flow, and retained a diminished electrophysiological response to light. The result therefore supports redundant contributions of Op1 and Op2 to this CO2-activated, light-dependent target-attraction behavior. It does not show that either receptor is individually necessary, that the double mutants are completely blind, or that olfactory tracking, close-range heat sensing, landing, probing, and biting are abolished.

source_provenance.locator: Results, Figure 2 and Supplementary Figure S2: op1/op2 double-mutant CO2-stimulated target attraction was nearly eliminated at 1000 and 100 lux and abolished at 25 and 5 lux; Results, Figure 3 and Supplementary Figure S3: normal human-odor tracking; Results, Figure 4 and Supplementary Figure S4: phototaxis, walking optomotor response, and ERG

topics.answer: No. The study supports a carbon-dioxide-gated preference among contrast-matched visual objects, not an unconditional preference for human-labeled red. Its attraction endpoint was the time a tracked trajectory spent around the test object relative to an evenly reflecting white control; it was not landing, probing, or biting. During filtered air, only 1-4% of mosquitoes investigated the objects. Carbon dioxide increased flight and object investigation, and attraction ceased after the plume stopped. With approximate object contrast matched, females preferred 600 and 660 nm objects, but they also preferred 496 nm; 437, 452, 510, and 520 nm were not more attractive than the white control. The pattern therefore cannot be reduced to long wavelength alone or to an unconditional mosquito concept of red. The experiments also omitted close-range heat, water vapor, or skin volatiles, so they do not establish consequences for landing or biting, human protection, or field repellency.

Aedes aegypti host seeking is multimodal, but the cues are not fully interchangeable. The cited primary studies directly support integration of carbon dioxide, human odor, and thermal infrared. They also show that op1/op2 double-mutant carbon-dioxide-stimulated visual target attraction was nearly eliminated at 1000 and 100 lux and abolished at 25 and 5 lux while odor tracking remained normal, and that carbon dioxide and a blood meal changed visual tracking. The thermal-infrared paper identifies close-range moisture and convective body heat as contributing when mosquitoes are very near skin. It distinguishes convective heat, which is sensed at less than 10 cm, from thermal infrared, which can provide a mid-range directional cue; neither is interchangeable with carbon dioxide, odor, or vision. The cited experiments do not directly manipulate humidity or moisture within this multimodal cue combination. That is a source gap, so the degree to which humidity is redundant with odor, vision, or heat is not established here. Cue weighting depends on physiological state, light level, and assay geometry; distinguish activation, long-range orientation, landing, and biting.

Aedes aegypti has daily host-seeking patterns, but the AeCyc knockout was broadly pleiotropic: it changed locomotor rhythms, reduced host-odor response, increased blood-feeding propensity, reduced and delayed egg hatching, affected later development, reduced adult survival, and reduced short-window mating success. Because the mutation changed both behavior and general fitness, its host-response phenotype cannot be assigned solely to a time-of-day repellent effect. Test the candidate in intact mosquitoes at a fixed time after lights-on or balance treatments across time blocks while holding light-dark entrainment, age, feeding state, and temperature consistent. Measure baseline locomotion, host-odor response, landing or biting, egg hatching and development, survival, and mating separately so a general deficit is not mislabeled as repellent sensitivity. The knockout is mechanistic evidence about the clock, not a repellent-efficacy test, and it does not supply the exact normal hourly effect size for every wild population.

source_provenance.title: Impact of disabled circadian clock on yellow fever mosquito Aedes aegypti fitness and behaviors
source_provenance.locator: Abstract and Results: AeCyc knockout and clock-gene expression; locomotor activity, host-odor response, blood-feeding, egg-hatching, development, survival, and mating phenotypes

topics.answer: Yes, under specific laboratory conditions. In one direct repellent study, previous DEET exposure was followed by reduced behavioral repellency when Aedes aegypti were tested three hours later, together with a reduced electroantennogram response to DEET. A separate associative learning study trained females with 10 pairings of a 60-second odor presentation and mechanical shock separated by 2-minute intervals, then found learned avoidance of mammalian-associated odorants 24 hours later, but not the same change for the floral odor linalool; dopamine signaling was implicated in that olfactory learning. The DEET result is short-term pre-exposure evidence, while the shock experiment is associative learning. The DEET result does not establish long-term persistence or field product failure, and the learning experiment does not show learning to every repellent. Habituation, sensitization, learned association, sensory adaptation, innate repellency, and toxic impairment remain separate explanations.

Population background can change a result enough to reverse the practical interpretation. At the same 0.00852% initial transfluthrin loading on paper, non-contact escape was 5.0% in the laboratory USDA strain and 54.9% in the field NON population. That is one assay and one population comparison; the loading is the initial paper loading, not a measured airborne concentration at the mosquito. A separate resistance-selection study measured longevity, first-blood-meal feeding rate, egg output, and development. Selection background affected several life-history outcomes, but no pairwise feeding-rate difference was significant, and the study does not quantify repellent response by age, genotype, or resistance state. Report and test population, genotype or resistance phenotype, age, mating, and feeding state across relevant backgrounds. Do not assume one colony represents the species or that insecticide resistance changes repellency in one fixed direction.

Use the open-field result for the next go/no-go decision if the intended product must protect people outdoors. The hessian transfluthrin emanators reduced Aedes aegypti landing by about 50-60% in large-cage semi-field tests across the tested doses, collection methods, placements, and mosquito sources, but two full-field experiments with wild Aedes aegypti found negligible landing reductions. The semi-field result preserves evidence of a context-dependent biological effect, but it should not override the result in the intended-use setting. The results should not be averaged. The authors judged moderate pyrethroid resistance unlikely to explain the field shortfall. The endpoint was human landing, not biting, infection, disease transmission, or duration of personal protection. Resolve the mismatch by matching formulation and loading across settings while measuring airborne transfluthrin concentration over distance and time, airflow and weather, emanator placement, mosquito population background, human landing and biting separately, and exposure safety. Potential inhalation exposure was a concern at the highest 15 g loading.

In the close-proximity odor assay, Aedes aegypti and Culex quinquefasciatus showed significant repellency to lemongrass oil, PMD, eugenol, and DEET. Anopheles coluzzii showed significant repellency to lemongrass oil and PMD; DEET was not significantly repellent, and eugenol produced a weak response that was not significantly different from paraffin oil (P=0.08). That is not the same as zero movement or no response by every mosquito. Each test used one resting female, filter paper about 0.5 cm away, and a 30-second observation window. This is direct odor-only, close-range movement evidence, not human-odor masking, landing, biting, complete protection time, or field efficacy. Do not transfer an Anopheles result to Aedes or infer a universal receptor; test the intended Aedes population at the intended vapor exposure, then confirm host-seeking and protection endpoints.

Aedes aegypti did not show one universal host preference across the 27 sub-Saharan African collection sites: laboratory odor-choice behavior varied widely among populations. In the final ecological model, dry-season intensity and regional human population density explained 83% of the geographic variation, and human preference was associated with shared human-specialist ancestry. Those predictors are associations, not proof that either factor alone causes preference. Repellent development should include multiple relevant populations and keep laboratory human-versus-animal odor choice separate from field blood meals, landing, and biting.

Female Aedes aegypti showed stable, large differences in attraction to odor collected on nylon sleeves worn by the same people repeatedly over months to years. More-attractive donors tended to have higher abundances of several skin-derived carboxylic acids, and a separate validation cohort reproduced the overall association while retaining individual exceptions. Mosquitoes lacking ionotropic-receptor co-receptors showed lower overall attraction to human odor but still distinguished relatively high- from low-attractiveness donors, so those receptors and acids do not by themselves explain the whole preference. Treat donor identity and sampling day as assay variables: repeat collections, balance donor order, include multiple donors, and keep odor-sample choice separate from landing, biting, and protection endpoints. The acid result is an association, not proof that one acid causes the complete phenotype or that changing it will create an effective repellent.

Female Aedes aegypti showed the strongest preference for a roughly 34 C infrared source when carbon dioxide (CO2) and human odor were also present. Infrared, CO2, or human odor alone produced little host-seeking in this assay. TRPA1 at the antennal tip was required for infrared detection, and two co-expressed opsins supported responses at lower radiant intensities. The study separates radiant infrared from conductive or convective heat, and it measured female host-seeking rather than biting or human protection. Infrared is therefore a plausible component of host location, not a validated stand-alone repellent target or proof that blocking it alone protects people.

The transfluthrin paper reported initial loading on treated papers but says in its Discussion that the active ingredient remaining on those papers at test time was not chemically determined. Its assay methods and reported results also provide no chamber-air concentration or plume-exposure measurement. The citronella paper measured thermal properties, arm-in-cage protection time, skin permeation, and mass balance; it interpreted the longer protection as prolonged release but did not directly measure a volatile-emission curve or chamber-air concentration. Those findings support keeping source loading, retained source mass, inferred release, measured emission, and measured mosquito exposure separate, but neither paper supplies a universal reporting standard. As an R&D reporting recommendation, record active ingredient and formulation, applied mass and area, source age, retained source mass when measured, evaporation or release rate over time when measured, chamber volume and ventilation, air-sampling method and airborne concentration when measured, source-to-mosquito distance, time, temperature, humidity, and airflow. Keep loading, retained mass, emission, transport, measured concentration, distance, and behavior separate; do not convert applied mass to milligrams per cubic meter without validated release and transport measurements. Taken together, these two papers do not provide one complete product-specific package spanning carrier, application, spatial delivery, measured release rate, and measured air concentration. That is a source gap within these two cited papers, not evidence that those product properties are absent.

Official EPA guidance for human-skin repellent trials says to wash the test limbs with unscented detergent, rinse, and dry them; avoid alcohol, tobacco, and scented products for at least twelve hours before and throughout the test; avoid activities that increase perspiration; and avoid abrading, rubbing, touching, or wetting the treated area. It also says to prevent cross-contamination and apply a consistent standard dose with uniform coverage. These are participant-preparation and treatment-integrity controls, not proof that all person-to-person differences in mosquito attraction have been removed. Tracking diet beyond alcohol, sleep, medication, menstrual or pregnancy status, baseline attractiveness, or skin microbiome may be useful R&D design choices when scientifically and ethically justified, but this cited section does not require them or establish how to stratify them. Pre-specify any such covariates and report them as study-design decisions rather than EPA requirements.

A landing-only arm-in-cage endpoint can establish reduced landings under the stated dose, mosquito density, exposure interval, participant, and cage conditions. It does not establish fewer probes or blood meals unless those outcomes are separately measured, and it does not by itself establish distance repellency, field performance, disease prevention, or durability through sweat, washing, abrasion, and weather. EPA guidance requires the study to define and score the chosen endpoint consistently. In tracking experiments, four topical repellents reduced bites while mosquitoes still made brief skin contact, so no bite does not mean no approach or contact. Preserve landing, contact, probing, biting, and blood feeding as separate endpoints.

WHO and EPA guidance provide standardized baseline efficacy and complete-protection-time methods for human-skin repellents. EPA's baseline subject controls explicitly avoid perspiration, abrasion, rubbing, touching, and wetting the treated area, so a baseline test does not establish protection after those stresses. The cited official guidance does not provide a validated sunlight or ultraviolet challenge protocol or a universal multi-stress durability matrix; that is a source gap. For product R&D, a challenge design could use separate, pre-specified sweat or water, washing and drying, rubbing or abrasion, exploratory sunlight or ultraviolet, and relevant temperature arms, each with an unchallenged time-matched control. That is an R&D challenge design, not a source-standardized protocol. Repeat landing or bite protection after each challenge; retained active ingredient or measured release is supporting evidence and cannot substitute for behavioral protection.

First separate three levels: ordinary avoidance is the intended behavioral response; reduced sensitivity is a measured behavioral phenotype; and physiological resistance requires evidence of an altered physiological mechanism. As an operational decision rule, require repeatable reduced sensitivity at a standardized dose or air concentration plus inheritance evidence before calling the phenotype inherited resistance; this is not a universal definition. Heritable behavioral insensitivity does not by itself prove an altered physiological mechanism because behavioral sensitivity can also be inherited. In the direct transfluthrin study, selective mating over nine generations produced an insensitive SRA- line, the decline was significant by generation F4, and an experimental cross between SRA- females and wild-type males restored spatial-repellent sensitivity and insecticide susceptibility. The association with reduced insecticide susceptibility supports a link, but altered uptake, metabolism, target-site action, or neural processing would need direct measurement before naming a physiological mechanism. A separate thymol- and permethrin-selection study measured longevity, first-blood-meal feeding rate, egg output, and development after selection. No pairwise feeding-rate difference was significant; the selected lines did show other life-history differences, including fecundity and development effects. Those are life-history outcomes after selection; the study does not measure repellent response in a standardized spatial-sensitivity assay or identify the physiological mechanism. A separate treated-clothing study also shows why kdr or insecticide-resistance status cannot automatically be equated with repellent failure. Insecticide resistance and repellent response cannot be assumed equivalent across compounds, formulations, routes, populations, or doses.

The PxylOR11 study linked three evidence levels without establishing receptor necessity. PxylOR11 was enriched in antennae and was expressed more strongly in female than male antennae. In Xenopus oocytes, PxylOR11 with Orco responded to benzyl alcohol, salicylaldehyde, and phenylacetaldehyde. The same three aromatic compounds elicited electroantennogram responses, with larger responses in females, and repelled females in dual-choice bioassays. Together, those molecular, antennal, and behavioral results support a role for PxylOR11 in the response. The study did not use a receptor knockout, so it did not show that PxylOR11 is necessary for repellency. It also did not establish an airborne field dose, persistence, reduced crop infestation or damage, improved yield, or commercial crop protection.

topics.answer: The PxylOR16 study supplies receptor-level causal evidence for one odor and one ecological context. PxylOR16 responded sensitively to heptanal, and both larvae and adults avoided heptanal. CRISPR/Cas9 knockout experimentally confirmed that PxylOR16 mediates that avoidance. The study traced heptanal to cuticular volatiles of the parasitoid Cotesia vestalis, rather than identifying it as a host-plant cue. This result does not prove that heptanal will repel all DBM populations or life stages under crop-field plume conditions. It also does not establish persistence, crop safety, reduced egg laying or larval establishment, lower crop damage, improved yield, or commercial field crop protection.

topics.answer: Three kinds of direct Plutella xylostella evidence are available. First, adult antennal transcriptomes catalogued 54 odorant receptors, 16 ionotropic receptors, 7 gustatory receptors, 15 chemosensory proteins, 24 odorant-binding proteins, and 2 sensory-neuron membrane proteins, with some sex-biased and tissue-specific expression. That is a candidate inventory, not functional proof. Second, PxylOR11 responded to benzyl alcohol, salicylaldehyde, and phenylacetaldehyde in Xenopus oocytes; the same compounds elicited antennal responses and repelled females in dual-choice assays. Those linked molecular, physiological, and behavioral measurements support a role for PxylOR11, but the study did not use a receptor knockout and did not test field crop protection. Third, PxylOR16 responded sensitively to heptanal, larvae and adults avoided heptanal, and CRISPR/Cas9 knockout confirmed that the receptor mediates that avoidance. The heptanal was traced to cuticular volatiles of the parasitoid Cotesia vestalis, not to a host plant. Neither receptor study established a dose-to-plume-to-yield chain, population-wide reliability, persistence under field weather, or commercial repellent efficacy.

topics.answer: The direct DBM study compared two strains with different levels of physiological insecticide resistance and measured behavior separately in females and larvae. In three-choice oviposition assays, females from the single-resistance strain laid more eggs on water-treated leaves than on leaves with complete gamma-cyhalothrin or spinetoram coverage. Females from the double-resistance strain avoided complete gamma-cyhalothrin coverage but did not change oviposition in response to spinetoram. Single-resistance larvae increased movement and avoided treated leaf areas for both insecticides, whereas double-resistance larvae decreased movement and did not avoid the treated areas. The result is compound-, strain-, and life-stage-specific. It supports the conclusion that physiological resistance level and behavioral avoidance can vary independently and may not share the same selection pressures. It does not identify the sensory receptor, neural circuit, genetic variant, or metabolic mechanism responsible for the behavior, and it does not show that greater physiological resistance always produces more or less avoidance in field populations.

topics.answer: Direct Plutella xylostella evidence supports several different layers, which should not be collapsed into one mechanism. An adult antennal transcriptome identified 54 odorant receptors, 16 ionotropic receptors, 7 gustatory receptors, 15 chemosensory proteins, 24 odorant-binding proteins, and 2 sensory-neuron membrane proteins; sex-biased and tissue-specific expression makes these candidates, but the transcriptome alone does not identify their ligands or prove their behavioral functions. PxylOR11 has direct functional evidence: it was antennally enriched, responded to benzyl alcohol, salicylaldehyde, and phenylacetaldehyde in Xenopus oocytes, and those compounds produced antennal responses and repelled females in dual-choice tests. PxylOR16 has stronger causal evidence for one cue: it responded sensitively to heptanal, larvae and adults avoided heptanal, and CRISPR/Cas9 knockout confirmed that PxylOR16 mediates the avoidance; the study traced heptanal to a parasitoid wasp rather than a host plant. Resistance-related behavior is a separate result. In choice assays with two physiologically resistant DBM strains, oviposition and larval movement or avoidance differed by strain, life stage, and whether leaves carried gamma-cyhalothrin or spinetoram. That study concluded that physiological resistance and behavioral avoidance need not share the same selection pressures; it does not establish that PxylOR11, PxylOR16, or another chemoreceptor caused the resistant-strain behavior. Genome studies add context by identifying expanded perception and detoxification gene families and population-level selection signatures, but they do not identify a causal repellent-resistance locus. Important uncertainties remain: receptor and strain effects need dose-response and population replication; laboratory avoidance has not established durable field crop protection; and no cited study links a chemoreceptor variant through airborne exposure, egg laying, larval establishment, crop damage, and yield. These are direct DBM findings and explicit gaps, not evidence that a receptor target or volatile is already a commercial repellent.

topics.answer: The evidence supports antennal detection and adult field attraction, not crop protection. The cited 2025 Plutella xylostella study identified eight broccoli volatiles that elicited antennal responses and found a three-component blend that attracted adults in field traps. It did not show that the blend reduced egg laying, larval establishment, feeding damage, or yield loss. A separate direct host-choice study measured orientation, landing, fecundity, and oviposition as distinct outcomes, which is why antennal response and trap catch cannot substitute for reproductive or crop endpoints. Test the blend in randomized choice and no-choice oviposition assays and then in matched field plots that separately count adults, eggs, larvae, leaf damage, and marketable yield. Until those downstream outcomes improve, the blend is a candidate attractant rather than a demonstrated crop repellent or crop-protection treatment.

The existing Plutella xylostella evidence establishes two separate stages, not their causal order. Reduced-wax or herbicide-treated surfaces received more eggs in a surface-choice study, while larva-induced host-plant volatiles attracted females in a Y-tube that did not measure landing, contact, or egg deposition. To determine how those cues combine, run a crossed 2 x 2 experiment: induced versus control headspace, delivered at a measured and matched flow, by wax-intact versus reduced-wax Brassica surfaces that are otherwise visually and chemically matched. Generate the headspace separately from the test plant so larval damage does not also alter its surface. Include clean-air and sham wax-manipulation controls, randomize sides and blocks, and standardize mated-female age and egg load. Record the sequence as separate endpoints: initial orientation, landing, first surface contact, residence time, total eggs, and egg allocation. Add matched no-choice arms for total egg output. A headspace effect on orientation followed by a wax effect after contact would support a staged sequence; an interaction would show that the final choice depends on both. This is a causal R&D design derived from the two studies, not an experiment either paper already performed.

Direct Plutella xylostella evidence separates host finding from egg laying. In one host-choice study, females oriented to Brassicaceae plant odors versus clean air but showed no preference when odors from three Brassicaceae species were presented together. Mixed cropping increased takeoff and reduced both field DBM population and oviposition compared with monoculture; landing also differed among host plants. Flying females use volatile plant cues to locate and land on hosts, while plant position and host-versus-nonhost identity also affect alighting. Direct oviposition tests found the isothiocyanates iberin and sulforaphane to be the most active volatile stimulants and also found activity in non-volatile plant fractions. Surface-choice experiments found more eggs on reduced-wax or herbicide-treated plants and on glossy rather than waxy strains, implicating epicuticular wax, although visual, chemical, and tactile mechanisms were not isolated. A separate study found that larva-induced host-plant volatiles attracted adult females in a Y-tube olfactory assay; it did not measure landing or egg deposition, so it cannot show that the attraction caused oviposition. Another study identified eight broccoli volatiles that elicited antennal responses: benzaldehyde, limonene, phenylacetaldehyde, acetophenone, linalool, 2-phenylethyl alcohol, methyl salicylate, and methyl 2-methoxybenzoate; field trapping supported a three-component blend. Antennal response, field trapping, attraction, landing, and egg deposition are not interchangeable endpoints, and antennal activity does not prove that a compound guides egg laying. Keep odor, vision, surface contact, landing, and oviposition separate when selecting or rejecting a cue.

Start with an assay ladder that separately measures adult orientation or landing, female oviposition, egg hatch, larval establishment and feeding, plant damage, beneficial-insect safety, and marketable yield or quality. Direct Plutella xylostella studies already demonstrate why the layers cannot be collapsed: one measured host orientation, landing, and egg laying; another separately counted adults, larvae, and leaf damage in broccoli; and the semiochemical study linked a four-choice adult assay to field population and beneficial-insect counts. Beneficial-insect abundance or harm is a separate safety gate for the candidate treatment, not a footnote to reduced moth landing. Those sources do not show that one early endpoint universally predicts marketable yield. The proposed order is a product-development design, and adult avoidance alone cannot establish reduced crop damage.

SWD and mosquito work can transfer assay principles and hypotheses: separate contact from non-contact exposure, measure dose and plume, use matched controls, and follow behavior with reproduction and damage endpoints. It cannot transfer efficacy. Direct Plutella xylostella studies already provide adult choice, host-location, oviposition, field population, larval-density, and leaf-damage endpoints, so a DBM program should build on those rather than claim the species is unstudied. The candidate repellent must still be tested directly in Plutella for non-contact orientation, contact effects, sex and life-stage response, oviposition, larval feeding and damage, dose response, toxicity, field plume behavior, and persistence. Keep the SWD and Aedes papers labeled as method analogies and the DBM papers labeled as focal-species evidence.

A return to normal activity at 30 minutes rules out neither reversible knockdown nor delayed mortality, and fewer landings alone does not prove spatial avoidance. Use matched non-contact and contact arms with carrier-only controls and a measured dose or release rate. In the non-contact arm, measure approach, landing, zone occupancy, exit, flight or walking speed, total distance, and immobility over time. During and after exposure, record knockdown separately, then follow the same moths for recovery at pre-specified intervals and delayed mortality such as 24 hours. In a separate contact-permitted arm, measure feeding or consumption directly, such as consumed leaf area, rather than inferring it from later reproduction. A selective reduction in approach or landing with preserved clean-air locomotion, no knockdown, and no excess mortality supports temporary spatial avoidance; general slowing, immobility, knockdown, or death supports toxic impairment. The host-finding paper directly measured flying and alighting. The citronella paper counted larval positions, later egg output from adults reared after larval treatment, larval mortality, and field larval abundance; it inferred reduced consumption but did not directly measure consumption or detailed locomotor kinematics. Neither source tested this exact candidate or 10-to-30-minute recovery design, so the proposed measurement set is an R&D recommendation rather than evidence that the compound is an effective repellent.

In the ROTH Plutella xylostella strain, period and timeless transcripts showed rhythms under entrained and free-running conditions, but measured adult locomotor activity was temperature-driven and light-suppressed rather than coupled to those clock-gene rhythms. A release schedule should therefore be tested against actual adult activity under the intended light and temperature pattern, not selected from transcript peaks alone. This laboratory strain does not establish field timing across populations, seasons, canopy conditions, or weather, so molecular and locomotor endpoints must remain separate and field timing needs direct validation.

The citronella study counted several distinct endpoints in Plutella xylostella: larval positions on treated and control sides of filter paper, eggs laid by adults after larval feeding treatment, larval mortality, and field larval abundance. It inferred reduced larval consumption from lower later egg output, but did not directly measure food consumption or detailed locomotor kinematics. Lower later egg output could instead reflect reduced ingestion, toxicity, developmental delay, survivor selection, poorer pupal or adult condition, altered mating, or a direct reproductive effect. Mortality was measured separately through 120 hours, so filter-paper position and later egg output should not be treated as pure spatial repellency or direct feeding measurements. Lower field abundance also cannot be assigned to repellency alone. A causal follow-up should separately measure no-contact orientation; direct treated-leaf consumption or leaf area; survival and knockdown; development time; pupal and adult condition; mating; total eggs and egg allocation; larval establishment; crop damage; and marketable yield. Compare vapor-matched no-contact and contact-permitted exposure arms so contact and airborne effects are not bundled. The reported Diadegma eucerophaga field observation is study- and species-specific, not general natural-enemy safety proof.

Across the eight cited primary studies, the missing baseline is not whether Plutella xylostella can respond to candidate repellents. Direct studies already report airflow or Y-tube avoidance, oviposition deterrence, larval toxicity or feeding effects, field trapping, and field population outcomes for Mikania micrantha oil, Angelica pubescens extract and compounds, non-host essential oils, and citronella. Those studies use source dose or airflow, but they do not provide one field-validated chain that measures airborne concentration at the moth, separates non-contact avoidance from contact effects and toxic impairment, and then links that same exposure to eggs, larvae, crop damage, and marketable yield. Within this cited evidence set, that quantified exposure-to-crop-protection chain is the highest-leverage gap. Close it with a randomized, blinded, staged study of one focal candidate and a known DBM-active positive control. First, use mated females and standardized Brassica plants in an airflow chamber with solvent and airflow controls, an upstream source physically isolated from moth and plant, and direct measurements of source release rate plus air sampling at the moth position over time to measure plume concentration. Record orientation, landing, zone occupancy, flight, knockdown, recovery, and mortality separately across a dose series. To estimate the added effect of contact, deliver the same measured candidate headspace to both arms, then compare access to a vehicle surface with access to a candidate-residue surface while confirming equivalent air concentration at the moth in both arms. A barrier/no-barrier implementation is acceptable only if the barrier does not change the measured plume. Do not label a volatile-treated surface contact-only; without vapor matching it cannot isolate contact. Carry those vapor-matched exposures into choice and no-choice oviposition assays, measuring total eggs as well as allocation, survival, larval establishment, and feeding damage. Then carry prespecified effective, non-toxic exposures into replicated semi-field or field plots and measure plume exposure, eggs, larval establishment, feeding damage, marketable yield or quality, persistence, weather, and non-target effects. Release rate alone is not airborne concentration, so measure both; a laboratory avoidance result alone would not close the field crop-protection gap. This prioritization and design are R&D recommendations, not evidence that one candidate already works.

Do not qualify an SWD exclusion net for a hot, fan-ventilated tunnel from a cool-reared, still-air result alone. The cited laboratory study tested nine commercial nets in a tubular device. It challenged passage at 0, 1.5, and 3.0 m/s fan-generated airflow while recording ambient temperature; temperature varied during the tests but was not experimentally controlled. Higher airflow and higher recorded temperatures were associated with lower efficacy, but the experiment did not establish a controlled temperature effect or a monotonic difference between 1.5 and 3.0 m/s. The laboratory flies also showed sexual dimorphism and an overall temperature-size relationship, although not every measured dimension was larger in the lower-temperature group. Some nets completely blocked passage in the tested combinations, but that is a conditional result rather than universal proof. Before deployment, cross the actual mesh with the tunnel's relevant airflow and temperature range and the colony or field population's sex and body-size distribution. Keep laboratory passage, tunnel ventilation and heat, season-long exclusion, fruit infestation, marketable yield, and durability as separate gates. The paper did not establish tunnel microclimate, crop protection, or yield under commercial deployment.

topics.answer: Do not advance the unchanged soil-applied hop-pellet formulation into a commercial SWD push program from the greenhouse result alone. In the 24-hour greenhouse cage study, HOP00 significantly reduced larval infestation relative to the untreated control, while HOP03 and HOP07 produced moderate but nonsignificant reductions; thymol and 1-octen-3-ol positive controls also reduced infestation. The controlled raspberry field trial did not reproduce a significant reduction for hop or for the dispenser-applied positive controls. Later commercial raspberry and blackberry trials found no significant reduction in larvae in fruit or trapped-fly density in hop-treated plots. Treat release rate, plume and upper-canopy coverage, persistence, wind dilution, and hop-variety headspace as formulation and exposure variables to measure, not as proven explanations for the field failure. The next gate should be a replicated field trial that measures delivered exposure and uses larvae in fruit as the primary efficacy endpoint, with trap counts kept separate. Stop advancing the unchanged formulation if it again fails to reduce larvae in fruit under the predefined field conditions, even if cage avoidance or adult trap counts look favorable. The study associated several headspace compounds with greenhouse efficacy but did not establish that any one compound caused repellency.

Do not use one fixed lower-south canopy sample as a validated season-long SWD infestation readout. The cited orchard study reared arthropods from individual sweet cherries from five successively ripening cultivars over seven weeks. It sampled interior and border rows and four canopy regions defined by top or bottom height crossed with north or south aspect. The observed pattern changed with time and density: at low SWD abundance, more flies occurred in the northernmost row and northern aspect; later, row, height, and aspect were homogeneous for part of the sampling period; at the highest density in the latest-ripening cultivar, more SWD occurred in the orchard center, lower canopy, and southern aspect than in some comparison positions. Early fruit samples contained single clutches without aggregation, while intraspecific aggregation increased as density rose. R&D inference, not a design validated by the paper: for a treatment trial, repeatedly stratify fruit-level infestation samples by row, canopy height, aspect, cultivar or ripening stage, and date. As a measurement-design recommendation, keep fruit-emergence infestation separate from adult trap counts and treatment efficacy; the paper did not compare or validate those endpoints. This one-orchard pattern does not establish a universal placement rule for a repellent or a universally best canopy zone.
